item.title: How can I avoid the World Cup?
item.description: It’s the longest ever. What’s a non-football fan to do?

item.title: The Musk exception on banking fees for SpaceX
item.description: Most companies are still likely to pay full price on smaller US deals as a prestige good

item.title: My returns have been exponential — no, seriously!
item.description: The speed at which my portfolio is rising in value is ridiculous

item.title: The CPG blues
item.description: Bad luck and bad decisions in consumer products

item.title: How much value is AI really creating?
item.description: Eye-opening changes to the speed and volume of work are not always translating into genuine productivity

item.title: Governments need to learn how to talk about debt
item.description: They must find a way to tell taxpayers how their money is spent

item.title: The big backlash over plans to drop US quarterly reporting demands
item.description: Enraged investors have sent flood of letters to SEC to express their opposition

item.title: How Britain could afford to pay for war
item.description: Keynes’s 1940 financing plan contains lessons for the current government

item.title: Men need a little vanity
item.description: Caring about how we look, within limits, helps everyone

item.title: Humanoids bring AI’s creative destruction to the shop floor
item.description: The anxiety of those whose jobs are at risk won’t be enough to stave off the march of the androids

item.title: The clematis and salvias to plant now for summer colour
item.description: Exhibitors at the RHS Chelsea Flower Show shared their top selections — and timely tips for looking after them

item.title: How to prepare for the next crisis
item.description: Laying the groundwork properly is important when companies face the unexpected

item.title: How to take advantage of disruption
item.description: Established industries are facing a fresh wave of digital transformation

item.title: Tech groups find a new way to monetise AI hype: selling volatility
item.description: Elevated convertible bond issuance is, at heart, a response to the market being twitchier than usual

item.title: SpaceX IPO shows Musk’s genius is in mythmaking
item.description: How exactly the company plans to support its gargantuan valuation is not obvious

item.title: The coming equity surge will test the US bull run
item.description: Mega IPOs and share offerings challenge the appetite for AI stocks

item.title: The case for raising UK interest rates is growing
item.description: The Bank of England should proactively lean against second-round effects from the energy shock

item.title: Europe is finally flexing its innovation muscles
item.description: The new EU tech sovereignty package shows a welcome shift from defensive regulation

item.title: The Gulf states and Ukraine need each other
item.description: Countries in Iran’s line of fire need to shore up their drone defences, and Kyiv is in desperate need of funding

item.title: What if Andy Burnham loses?
item.description: Defeat would encourage Labour to believe the next election will be won by uniting voters on the left

item.title: Governments are scared of tackling the immigration dilemma
item.description: Rich economies need workers but their voters don’t like migrants

item.title: A Labour battle for ideas would be a public service
item.description: An anti-intellectual leadership contest is a missed opportunity

item.title: Japan’s bonds
item.description: Plus, India’s wobble

item.title: America’s ‘other’ economy tells a different growth story
item.description: Investors should pay attention to the struggles of the consumer packaged goods industry

item.title: How investors need to adapt to a new era of ‘spikeflation’
item.description: Diversification is the first defence against uncertainty and investors should seek inflation hedges like commodities
